claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 4 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 6 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about your child (or children).**

How old is the child you're claiming Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children, we can go through each one. Let's start with the first child - what is their name and age?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi! Yes, I'd like to check about Child Benefit for my two children.

My oldest is Alex, who's 6 years old. And then there's Blake, who's 4.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Lovely! Thank you for sharing that. Let me check the eligibility for both Alex (6 years old) and Blake (4 years old).

Since both children are **under 16 years old**, they automatically meet the age requirement for Child Benefit. Now I need to check a few more things.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Since Alex and Blake are only 6 and 4 years old, they wouldn't be in paid employment. So they meet this requirement - children under 16 are not expected to be working.

Let me continue to the next check:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:** Are Alex and Blake receiving any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

(Given their young ages, this is very unlikely, but I need to confirm.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them get any benefits. They're just young kids!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That makes sense! At 6 and 4 years old, they wouldn't be receiving those kinds of benefits. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Given that Alex is 6 and Blake is 4, they're far too young to be in any apprenticeship programme. So they easily pass this check too!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status.

**Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **any one** of these applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have **pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have **pre-settled status** and are actively seeking work
5. You have **pre-settled status** with sufficient resources to support yourself
6. You have **pre-settled status** and are a student with sufficient resources
7. You have **pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You're a **Crown servant** living abroad

Do any of these apply to you? For most people, it's simply that they normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm based here in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Living in the UK (Manchester) means you meet the residency requirement. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:** Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?

You're considered responsible if **any one** of these applies:

1. **You live with the children**
2. **You contribute to their upkeep** (e.g., paying for food, clothes, pocket money, housing) at least the same amount as Child Benefit
3. **You pay maintenance** for them under a court order or agreement

Do any of these apply to you for both children?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Well, Alex lives at home with me, so that's straightforward.

Blake doesn't live with me though - he lives with his mum. I do help out a bit with things like clothes and bits and pieces for him, but it's not a huge amount.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that - this is helpful information.

For **Alex** (who lives with you), that's straightforward - you clearly meet the responsibility requirement.

For **Blake**, the situation is a bit different. Since Blake lives with his mum, the key question is whether your contributions towards his upkeep are **at least equal to the Child Benefit amount**.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Let me ask a bit more about your contributions for Blake.

**For Blake:** To qualify based on contributing to his upkeep, your contributions (whether in cash or in kind - like clothes, presents, food, etc.) need to be **at least equal to the Child Benefit amount** (currently around £25.60 per week for a first/only child, or £16.95 for additional children).

Would you say your contributions towards Blake's care add up to roughly **£17 or more per week** (or equivalent over a month/year)? This could include:
- Clothes
- Presents
- Food
- Pocket money
- Any other items for his care

Also, is Blake's mum already claiming Child Benefit for him? This is important because only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
I give about £20 a week to help with things for Blake - clothes, bits for school, that sort of thing.

As for his mum claiming - yes, I believe she does already claim Child Benefit for him since he lives with her.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information - this is really important for determining eligibility.

**For Alex:** Everything looks good. He lives with you, so you clearly meet the responsibility requirement.

**For Blake:** While your £20 per week contribution does meet the financial threshold, there's a complication: **Blake's mum is already claiming Child Benefit for him**.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This confirms what I suspected. Since Blake's mum is already claiming Child Benefit for him, **only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child at any time**.

The rules are:
- If Blake **lives with his mum**, she has priority for the claim
- You could only claim for Blake if she agreed to transfer the claim to you, but since Blake lives with her, she would typically have the stronger claim

Let me now complete the assessment for **Alex**, who you clearly can claim for:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: **For Alex:** Is he living with you at home under normal circumstances? Or is he currently in hospital, in care, or in a fostering arrangement?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is just at home with me - nothing special, just normal circumstances.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final question for Alex:** Is the local council **NOT** paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?

In other words, are you fully responsible for housing and looking after Alex without council financial support for his accommodation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - I'm looking after Alex myself. The council isn't paying anything towards him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I now have all the information I need to give you my assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Assessment Complete

Here's the outcome for your two children:

### ✅ Alex (age 6) — **ELIGIBLE**

Great news! You can claim Child Benefit for Alex. He meets all the eligibility criteria:
- He's under 16 years old
- He lives with you
- No one else is claiming for him
- The council isn't contributing to his care

### ❌ Blake (age 4) — **NOT ELIGIBLE for you to claim**

Unfortunately, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake because:
- His mum is already claiming Child Benefit for him
- Only one person can claim for each child
- Since Blake lives with her, she has priority for the claim

Your £20 weekly contributions are appreciated, but they don't override the fact that Blake lives with his mum and she's already the claimant.

---

## 📝 Next Steps for Alex

To claim Child Benefit for Alex, you should:

1. **Apply online** or by post using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** (or adoption certificate if applicable)

**Important:** If you or your partner earns over **£60,000**, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge. You can still claim, but some or all of the benefit may be recovered through tax.

Payments are usually made **every 4 weeks** (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits).

👉 Apply here: [www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

Do you have any questions about the assessment or the application process?

reasonings: Alex (6 years old) meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit: (1) He is under 16 years old, so automatically qualifies on age grounds. (2) He is not working or receiving benefits in his own right. (3) He is not in an apprenticeship. (4) You (the claimant) normally live in the UK. (5) You are responsible for Alex as he lives with you. (6) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. (7) He is living at home under normal circumstances. (8) The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (4 years old) meets most eligibility criteria, however you cannot claim Child Benefit for him because his mum is already claiming Child Benefit for him. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at any one time. Since Blake lives with his mum, she has priority for the claim. Your contributions of £20 per week, while meeting the financial threshold for 'responsibility', do not override the fact that Blake lives with his mum and she is already the claimant.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex, who lives with you. However, you cannot claim for Blake because his mother is already claiming Child Benefit for him and he lives with her, giving her priority for the claim.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate

Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

(2) He is not working or receiving benefits in his own right. (3) He is not in an apprenticeship. (4) You (the claimant) normally live in the UK. (5) You are responsible for Alex as he lives with you. (6) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. (7) He is living at home under normal circumstances. (8) The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (4 years old) meets most eligibility criteria, however you cannot claim Child Benefit for him because his mum is already claiming Child Benefit for him. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at any one time. Since Blake lives with his mum, she has priority for the claim. Your contributions of £20 per week, while meeting the financial threshold for 'responsibility', do not override the fact that Blake lives with his mum and she is already the claimant.
